%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-plan" data-visual-plan-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
# Signal Power

> **Section Objective:** Learn how signal power measures the long-term average size of signals whose total energy is not finite.

---

## Concepts In This Section

- Energy signal
- Power signal
- Time-average power
- Periodic-signal power
- RMS value
- Sinusoid power

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="book_image" data-teaching-role="comparison_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
![Figure 1.1](/figures/page-066-figure_1_1-1.png)
*Figure 1.1 contrasts a finite-energy transient signal with a finite-power signal that persists over time.*
<div class="lesson-figure-description">The top plot shows a signal that rises from zero, peaks, and decays back toward zero — its total squared area can be finite, making it an energy signal. The bottom plot shows a continuing oscillatory signal that never dies out — total energy keeps accumulating indefinitely, but the long-term average power can still be finite. Students should focus on what each signal does as \(|t|\) becomes large: the top signal shrinks toward zero, while the bottom signal keeps oscillating at roughly the same amplitude.</div>

$$E_x = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} |x(t)|^2\,dt$$
***Eq. (1.1) — Signal Energy.** This formula adds up the squared magnitude of \(x(t)\) over all time.

- \(x(t)\): the signal value at time \(t\)
- \(|x(t)|^2\): squared magnitude — always non-negative, so no cancellation
- The integral accumulates that squared size from \(t = -\infty\) to \(t = +\infty\)

**Exam trigger:** Use energy when the signal decays toward zero as \(|t| \to \infty\). A finite pulse is nonzero only over a limited interval, so its total squared area is finite.

#### Common Misuse
Do not use the signed integral \(\int x(t)\,dt\). Positive and negative parts can cancel, hiding the true signal size. Always square first.*

$$P_x = \lim_{T\to\infty}\frac{1}{T}\int_{-T/2}^{T/2}|x(t)|^2\,dt$$
***Eq. (1.2) — Signal Power.** Power is the long-term average of squared magnitude — not the total squared area.

- \(T\): length of the averaging window
- \([-T/2,\, T/2]\): the symmetric window centered at zero
- \(\frac{1}{T}\): divides the accumulated squared magnitude by window length to form an average
- The limit asks: what does that average settle to as the window grows without bound?

**When to use it:** Total energy is infinite, but the average squared size may still settle to a finite value. Periodic signals and other never-ending bounded signals are the main candidates.

#### Common Misuse
Forgetting the factor \(1/T\) turns the calculation back into energy, not power.*

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="interactive_demo" data-teaching-role="concept_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div><div class="kc-interactive-demo" data-demo-b64=""></div>%%KC_END%%

$$P_x = \frac{1}{T_0}\int_{t_0}^{t_0+T_0}|x(t)|^2\,dt$$
***Periodic-Signal Power Shortcut.** For a signal that repeats with period \(T_0\), the long-term average equals the average over any single complete period.

- \(T_0\): the period (length of one full repeated pattern)
- \(t_0\): any convenient starting time — the result is the same regardless of where you start, because the pattern repeats
- \(\frac{1}{T_0}\): divides by the period length to form the average

**When to use it:** The problem states or shows a repeating waveform. One full period contains all the information needed.

**Exam trigger:** The problem gives a period or the waveform is visually periodic.

#### Common Misuse
Integrating over one period but forgetting to divide by \(T_0\) gives total energy over one period, not average power.*

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="book_image" data-teaching-role="example_support" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
![Fig. 1.2](/figures/page-067-fig_12-1.png)
*Fig. 1.2 compares a decaying signal measured by energy with a periodic signal measured by power.*
<div class="lesson-figure-description">Part (a) shows a signal that is zero for \(t &lt; -1\), jumps to amplitude 2 at \(t = -1\), and then follows a decaying exponential \(2e^{-t/2}\) for \(t \geq 0\). Because the signal decays toward zero as \(t \to \infty\), total squared area is finite and energy is the right measure; the textbook result is \(E_x = 8\). Part (b) shows a repeating sawtooth waveform that rises linearly from \(-1\) to \(1\) and drops abruptly, with period \(T_0 = 2\). Because the signal persists forever without decaying, total energy is infinite but average power over one period is finite; the textbook results are \(P_x = 1/3\) and rms value \(1/\sqrt{3}\). Students should notice that the key difference is what each signal does as time extends far in either direction.</div>

$$x_{\mathrm{rms}} = \sqrt{P_x}$$
***RMS Value.** The root-mean-square value is the square root of signal power, so signal power equals the square of the rms value.

- \(x_{\mathrm{rms}}\): root-mean-square size — a single number summarizing average signal strength
- Use it when the problem asks for rms after computing power, or when comparing average signal levels

**Representative result for sinusoids:** For \(x(t) = C\cos(\omega_0 t + \theta)\), averaging \(\cos^2\) over a full period gives \(1/2\), so:

$$P_x = \frac{C^2}{2}, \quad x_{\mathrm{rms}} = \frac{|C|}{\sqrt{2}}$$

#### Common Misuse
Do not use the peak amplitude \(C\) as the rms value of a sinusoid. The rms value is \(|C|/\sqrt{2}\), which is smaller than \(C\) by a factor of \(\sqrt{2}\).*

### Important Formula: scale, sign, rms, and same-frequency sums

For a scaled signal \(a x(t)\):
$$P_{a x}=a^2P_x,\qquad (a x)_{\rm rms}=|a|x_{\rm rms}$$

A sign flip does not change power:
$$P_{-x}=P_x$$

For a sinusoid plus dc or another sinusoid, do not add peak amplitudes. Average the square. If two equal-frequency cosines have phases \(\theta_1,\theta_2\), the cross term survives:

$$P=\frac{C_1^2+C_2^2+2C_1C_2\cos(\theta_1-\theta_2)}{2}$$

For a product such as \((10+2\sin 3t)\cos 10t\), expand the square carefully or use averages and orthogonality. The constant envelope and sinusoidal envelope both contribute to average power.

#### Exam Trap

If frequencies are different over a full common period, many cross terms average to 0. If frequencies are equal, the phase difference can keep a cross term.

---
**📌 Key Takeaways**
- **Energy formula (Eq. 1.1):** $$E_x = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} |x(t)|^2\,dt$$ Use when the signal decays toward zero as \(|t| \to \infty\).
- **Power formula (Eq. 1.2):** $$P_x = \lim_{T\to\infty}\frac{1}{T}\int_{-T/2}^{T/2}|x(t)|^2\,dt$$ Use when total energy is infinite but the long-term average squared size may be finite.
- **Periodic-signal shortcut:** $$P_x = \frac{1}{T_0}\int_{t_0}^{t_0+T_0}|x(t)|^2\,dt$$ One full period is enough; any starting time \(t_0\) works.
- **RMS value:** $$x_{\mathrm{rms}} = \sqrt{P_x}$$
- **Sinusoid result:** For \(x(t) = C\cos(\omega_0 t + \theta)\): $$P_x = \frac{C^2}{2}, \quad x_{\mathrm{rms}} = \frac{|C|}{\sqrt{2}}$$
- **Classification rule:** A signal that decays to zero is an energy-signal candidate. A signal that persists periodically is a power-signal candidate.
- **Key trap:** The factor \(1/T\) in the power formula is what separates power from energy — never omit it.

*Next, we will use these size measures when analyzing signals and systems more broadly.*

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-quiz-plan" data-quiz-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
